[[File:Tokugawa Seiseiroku 001.jpg|thumb|350px| "En masse Attendance of Daimyo at [[:en:Edo Castle|Edo Castle]] on a Festive Day" from the Tokugawa Seiseiroku, [[:en:National Museum of Japanese History|National Museum of Japanese History]]]] '''''Sankin-kōtai''''' ({{langx|ja|参覲交代/参覲交替}}, now commonly written as {{langx|ja|参勤交代/参勤交替|lit=alternate attendance|label=none}}) was a policy of the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] during most of the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], created to control the [[daimyo]], the feudal lords of Japan, politically, and to keep them from attempting to overthrow the regime. It required most daimyo to alternate between living in their domain and in the shogunate's capital, [[:en:Edo|Edo]], every year. This made the daimyo subject to constant surveillance from the shogunate. This also forced the daimyo to have residences in both their domain and Edo. The cost of maintaining several lavish residences as well as the journeys to and from Edo was a constant drain on the finances of the daimyo, which greatly increased the shogunate's control over them and kept them militarily weak. The daimyo were also required to keep their wife and children in Edo permanently to act as hostages.
==History== [[:en:Toyotomi Hideyoshi|Toyotomi Hideyoshi]] had earlier established a similar practice of requiring his feudal lords to keep their wives and heirs at [[:en:Osaka Castle|Osaka Castle]] or the nearby vicinity as hostages to ensure their loyalty. Following the [[:en:Battle of Sekigahara|Battle of Sekigahara]] and the establishment of the Tokugawa Shogunate, this practice was continued at the new capital of Edo as a matter of custom. It was made compulsory for the ''[[:en:Tozama daimyō|Tozama daimyō]]s'' in 1635, and for the ''[[:en:Fudai daimyō|Fudai daimyō]]s'' from 1642. Aside from an eight-year period under the rule of [[:en:Tokugawa Yoshimune|Tokugawa Yoshimune]], the law remained in force until 1862.<ref name= Beasley>{{cite book | last = Beasley| first = William G | year = 1972 | title = The Meiji Restoration| url = https://archive.org/details/meijirestoration00beas| url-access = limited| publisher = Stamford University Press | isbn=0804708150 |pages=[https://archive.org/details/meijirestoration00beas/page/n30 17]–18}}</ref>
===Predecessors=== The ''sankin-kōtai'' system was a natural outgrowth of pre-existing practices which were expanded by the Tokugawa shogunate to further their own political interests.{{sfn|Tsukahira|1966|p=30}} Much of the reason the newly created shogunate could impose ''sankin-kōtai'' on the defeated ''daimyo'' with ease was due to these immediate predecessors.{{sfn|Tsukahira|1966|p=35}} ====Kamakura period==== The Tokugawa period philosopher [[:en:Kumazawa Banzan|Kumazawa Banzan]] wrote in his ''Daigaku wakumon'' that the [[:en:Kamakura shogunate|Kamakura shogunate]] had its own version of alternate attendance, which made ''[[:en:Gokenin|Gokenin]]'' visit the shogun's court in [[:en:Kamakura|Kamakura]] for fifty days once every three years. Kumazawa claimed that his account was based on records preserved by the descendants of ancient Kamakura families. Modern scholars have been unable to confirm the existence of such detailed rules, but there is evidence that, at least in principle, Kamakura period ''gokenin'' owed some kind of service at the shogun's court. The obligatory ''ōyuka'' service required ''gokenin'' to attend at the shogun's court for one month. They were also required to attend on special occasions such as comings of age, weddings, and funerals.{{sfn|Tsukahira|1966|pp=31-32}}
The principal duty of the ''gokenin'' was ''{{ill|ōban'yaku|ja|大番役|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/ōban'yaku|1=ōban'yaku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11261246}}'', which was periodic guard duty at [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto]] and Kamakura. It originated as a public duty of the warrior class, requiring performance of guard service in Kyoto in one out of every three years. In 1186, [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]] made this service mandatory for ''gokenin'' and reduced the duration to six months, and then later to three. In 1275, a similar arrangement was instituted at Kamakura, making ''gokenin'' in eastern Japan serve there for one month annually. Important ''gokenin'' who frequently performed guard service there maintained residences in Kamakura, even those from remoter regions in western Japan. It was also frequent practice for ''[[:en:Shugo|Shugo]]'' and ''[[:en:Jitō|Jitō]]'' to have their sons serve in the shogun's court, acting as hostages. In some cases, families were kept in Kamakura.{{sfn|Tsukahira|1966|pp=32-33}}
These obligations on the Kamakura ''gokenin'' had a limiting effect on local independence, like the more rigid ''sankin-kōtai'' system that emerged in the Edo period. Shimmi Kichiji claimed that the prolonged absence of the ''gokenin'' from their fiefs due to their obligatory attendance at the capital delayed the development of true feudalism in Japan.{{sfn|Tsukahira|1966|p=33}}
==Description== [[File:Kyosai Kiyomitsu 001c.jpg|thumb|Sightseers and merchants gazing at an entourage (sixth panel) from "Folding Screen Depicting Scenes of the Attendance of ''Daimyōs'' at Edo Castle", National Museum of Japanese History]] The details changed throughout the 26 decades of Tokugawa rule, but generally, the requirement was that the ''daimyōs'' of every [[:en:Han (Japan)|''han'']] move periodically between Edo and his fief, typically spending alternate years in each place. His wife and heir were required to remain in Edo as hostages while he was away. The expenditures necessary to maintain lavish residences in both places, and for the procession to and from Edo, placed financial strains on the daimyo, making them unable to wage war. The frequent travel of the daimyo encouraged road building and the construction of inns and facilities along the routes, generating economic activity.
There were a number of exceptions for certain ''fudai daimyōs'' in the vicinity of Edo, who were allowed to alternate their attendance in Edo every six months instead. Temporary exceptional dispensations were also occasionally granted due to illness or extreme extenuating circumstances.<ref name= Beasley/>
In principle, the ''sankin-kōtai'' was a military service to the shōgun. Each ''daimyō'' was required to furnish a number of soldiers ([[:en:Samurai|Samurai]]) in accordance with the ''[[:en:Kokudaka|Kokudaka]]'' assessment of his domain. These soldiers accompanied the ''daimyō'' on the processions to and from Edo.
With hundreds of ''daimyōs'' entering or leaving Edo each year, {{nihongo|processions|大名行列|daimyō-gyōretsu}} were almost daily occurrences in the shogunal capital. The main routes to the provinces were the ''[[:en:Kaidō|Kaidō]]''. Special lodgings, the {{nihongo|''honjin''|本陣}}, were available to ''daimyōs'' during their travels.
== Effects ==
=== Economic === The expense of maintaining lavish residences in both Edo and their domain as well as the costly annual journeys to and from Edo was disastrous for the daimyo and greatly enhanced the political control imposed by the ''sankin-kōtai'' system.{{sfn|Tsukahira|1966|p=81}} As the main source of money for most daimyo was mainly dependent on the rice surplus, which was sharply limited by the relative inflexibility of the productive capacity of the [[:en:Han system|''han'']], the incomes of the daimyo were unable to keep up with the increasing financial needs of the daimyo, forcing them to go into debt.{{sfn|Tsukahira|1966|pp=83-84}} ''Sankin-kōtai'' expenses regularly made up 70 to 80 percent of the total annual expenditures of daimyo. Economic programs, efforts to increase production, and special taxes were all attempted, but offered no permanent solution.{{sfn|Tsukahira|1966|p=101}} By the [[:en:Genroku|Genroku period]], practically every daimyo was deep in debt due to the extravagant spending universal during this period. Even the wealthiest feudatory, the [[Maeda clan]] of Kaga, began to encounter financial difficulties at this time.{{sfn|Tsukahira|1966|p=85}}
== Similar practices == [[:en:King of France|King]] [[:en:Louis XIV of France|Louis XIV of France]] instituted a similar practice upon the completion of his [[:en:Palace of Versailles|palace at Versailles]], requiring the [[:en:French nobility|French nobility]], particularly the ancient ''[[:en:Noblesse d'épée|Noblesse d'épée]]'' ("nobility of the sword") to spend six months of each year at the palace, for reasons similar to those of the Japanese shōguns. The nobles were expected to assist the king in his daily duties and state and personal functions, including meals, parties, and, for the privileged, [[:en:Levée (ceremony)|rising from and getting into bed]], bathing, and going to church.
==See also == * [[:en:Dutch missions to Edo|Dutch missions to Edo]] * [[:en:Ryukyuan missions to Edo|Ryukyuan missions to Edo]]
==References== {{reflist}}
== Bibliography ==
* {{Cite book |last=Tsukahira |first=Toshio |url=https://archive.org/details/feudalcontrolint0000tsuk |title=Feudal Control in Tokugawa Japan: the Sankin Kōtai System |publisher=East Asian Research Center, Harvard University |year=1966 |isbn=9780674299009 |url-access=registration}} * {{Cite book |last=Jansen |first=Marius B. |author-link=Marius B. Jansen |title=The Making of Modern Japan |title-link=The Making of Modern Japan |publisher=[[Harvard University Press]] |year=2000 |isbn=978-0-674-00991-2}} {{commonscat-inline}}

== Similar practices == [[:en:King of France|King]] [[:en:Louis XIV of France|Louis XIV of France]] instituted a similar practice upon the completion of his [[:en:Palace of Versailles|palace at Versailles]], requiring the [[:en:French nobility|French nobility]], particularly the ancient ''[[:en:Noblesse d'épée|Noblesse d'épée]]'' ("nobility of the sword") to spend six months of each year at the palace, for reasons similar to those of the Japanese shōguns. The nobles were expected to assist the king in his daily duties and state and personal functions, including meals, parties, and, for the privileged, [[:en:Levée (ceremony)|rising from and getting into bed]], bathing, and going to church.

[[File:Tokugawa Seiseiroku 001.jpg|thumb|350px| "En masse Attendance of Daimyo at [[:en:Edo Castle|Edo Castle]] on a Festive Day" from the Tokugawa Seiseiroku, [[:en:National Museum of Japanese History|National Museum of Japanese History]]]] '''''Sankin-kōtai''''' ({{langx|ja|参覲交代/参覲交替}}, now commonly written as {{langx|ja|参勤交代/参勤交替|lit=alternate attendance|label=none}}) was a policy of the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] during most of the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], created to control the [[daimyo]], the feudal lords of Japan, politically, and to keep them from attempting to overthrow the regime. It required most daimyo to alternate between living in their domain and in the shogunate's capital, [[:en:Edo|Edo]], every year. This made the daimyo subject to constant surveillance from the shogunate. This also forced the daimyo to have residences in both their domain and Edo. The cost of maintaining several lavish residences as well as the journeys to and from Edo was a constant drain on the finances of the daimyo, which greatly increased the shogunate's control over them and kept them militarily weak. The daimyo were also required to keep their wife and children in Edo permanently to act as hostages.
==History== [[:en:Toyotomi Hideyoshi|Toyotomi Hideyoshi]] had earlier established a similar practice of requiring his feudal lords to keep their wives and heirs at [[:en:Osaka Castle|Osaka Castle]] or the nearby vicinity as hostages to ensure their loyalty. Following the [[:en:Battle of Sekigahara|Battle of Sekigahara]] and the establishment of the Tokugawa Shogunate, this practice was continued at the new capital of Edo as a matter of custom. It was made compulsory for the ''[[:en:Tozama daimyō|Tozama daimyō]]s'' in 1635, and for the ''[[:en:Fudai daimyō|Fudai daimyō]]s'' from 1642. Aside from an eight-year period under the rule of [[:en:Tokugawa Yoshimune|Tokugawa Yoshimune]], the law remained in force until 1862.<ref name= Beasley>{{cite book | last = Beasley| first = William G | year = 1972 | title = The Meiji Restoration| url = https://archive.org/details/meijirestoration00beas| url-access = limited| publisher = Stamford University Press | isbn=0804708150 |pages=[https://archive.org/details/meijirestoration00beas/page/n30 17]–18}}</ref>
===Predecessors=== The ''sankin-kōtai'' system was a natural outgrowth of pre-existing practices which were expanded by the Tokugawa shogunate to further their own political interests.{{sfn|Tsukahira|1966|p=30}} Much of the reason the newly created shogunate could impose ''sankin-kōtai'' on the defeated ''daimyo'' with ease was due to these immediate predecessors.{{sfn|Tsukahira|1966|p=35}} ====Kamakura period==== The Tokugawa period philosopher [[:en:Kumazawa Banzan|Kumazawa Banzan]] wrote in his ''Daigaku wakumon'' that the [[:en:Kamakura shogunate|Kamakura shogunate]] had its own version of alternate attendance, which made ''[[:en:Gokenin|Gokenin]]'' visit the shogun's court in [[:en:Kamakura|Kamakura]] for fifty days once every three years. Kumazawa claimed that his account was based on records preserved by the descendants of ancient Kamakura families. Modern scholars have been unable to confirm the existence of such detailed rules, but there is evidence that, at least in principle, Kamakura period ''gokenin'' owed some kind of service at the shogun's court. The obligatory ''ōyuka'' service required ''gokenin'' to attend at the shogun's court for one month. They were also required to attend on special occasions such as comings of age, weddings, and funerals.{{sfn|Tsukahira|1966|pp=31-32}}
The principal duty of the ''gokenin'' was ''{{ill|ōban'yaku|ja|大番役|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/ōban'yaku|1=ōban'yaku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11261246}}'', which was periodic guard duty at [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto]] and Kamakura. It originated as a public duty of the warrior class, requiring performance of guard service in Kyoto in one out of every three years. In 1186, [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]] made this service mandatory for ''gokenin'' and reduced the duration to six months, and then later to three. In 1275, a similar arrangement was instituted at Kamakura, making ''gokenin'' in eastern Japan serve there for one month annually. Important ''gokenin'' who frequently performed guard service there maintained residences in Kamakura, even those from remoter regions in western Japan. It was also frequent practice for ''[[:en:Shugo|Shugo]]'' and ''[[:en:Jitō|Jitō]]'' to have their sons serve in the shogun's court, acting as hostages. In some cases, families were kept in Kamakura.{{sfn|Tsukahira|1966|pp=32-33}}
These obligations on the Kamakura ''gokenin'' had a limiting effect on local independence, like the more rigid ''sankin-kōtai'' system that emerged in the Edo period. Shimmi Kichiji claimed that the prolonged absence of the ''gokenin'' from their fiefs due to their obligatory attendance at the capital delayed the development of true feudalism in Japan.{{sfn|Tsukahira|1966|p=33}}
==Description== [[File:Kyosai Kiyomitsu 001c.jpg|thumb|Sightseers and merchants gazing at an entourage (sixth panel) from "Folding Screen Depicting Scenes of the Attendance of ''Daimyōs'' at Edo Castle", National Museum of Japanese History]] The details changed throughout the 26 decades of Tokugawa rule, but generally, the requirement was that the ''daimyōs'' of every [[:en:Han (Japan)|''han'']] move periodically between Edo and his fief, typically spending alternate years in each place. His wife and heir were required to remain in Edo as hostages while he was away. The expenditures necessary to maintain lavish residences in both places, and for the procession to and from Edo, placed financial strains on the daimyo, making them unable to wage war. The frequent travel of the daimyo encouraged road building and the construction of inns and facilities along the routes, generating economic activity.
There were a number of exceptions for certain ''fudai daimyōs'' in the vicinity of Edo, who were allowed to alternate their attendance in Edo every six months instead. Temporary exceptional dispensations were also occasionally granted due to illness or extreme extenuating circumstances.<ref name= Beasley/>
In principle, the ''sankin-kōtai'' was a military service to the shōgun. Each ''daimyō'' was required to furnish a number of soldiers ([[:en:Samurai|Samurai]]) in accordance with the ''[[:en:Kokudaka|Kokudaka]]'' assessment of his domain. These soldiers accompanied the ''daimyō'' on the processions to and from Edo.
With hundreds of ''daimyōs'' entering or leaving Edo each year, {{nihongo|processions|大名行列|daimyō-gyōretsu}} were almost daily occurrences in the shogunal capital. The main routes to the provinces were the ''[[:en:Kaidō|Kaidō]]''. Special lodgings, the {{nihongo|''honjin''|本陣}}, were available to ''daimyōs'' during their travels.
== Effects ==
=== Economic === The expense of maintaining lavish residences in both Edo and their domain as well as the costly annual journeys to and from Edo was disastrous for the daimyo and greatly enhanced the political control imposed by the ''sankin-kōtai'' system.{{sfn|Tsukahira|1966|p=81}} As the main source of money for most daimyo was mainly dependent on the rice surplus, which was sharply limited by the relative inflexibility of the productive capacity of the [[:en:Han system|''han'']], the incomes of the daimyo were unable to keep up with the increasing financial needs of the daimyo, forcing them to go into debt.{{sfn|Tsukahira|1966|pp=83-84}} ''Sankin-kōtai'' expenses regularly made up 70 to 80 percent of the total annual expenditures of daimyo. Economic programs, efforts to increase production, and special taxes were all attempted, but offered no permanent solution.{{sfn|Tsukahira|1966|p=101}} By the [[:en:Genroku|Genroku period]], practically every daimyo was deep in debt due to the extravagant spending universal during this period. Even the wealthiest feudatory, the [[Maeda clan]] of Kaga, began to encounter financial difficulties at this time.{{sfn|Tsukahira|1966|p=85}}
== Similar practices == [[:en:King of France|King]] [[:en:Louis XIV of France|Louis XIV of France]] instituted a similar practice upon the completion of his [[:en:Palace of Versailles|palace at Versailles]], requiring the [[:en:French nobility|French nobility]], particularly the ancient ''[[:en:Noblesse d'épée|Noblesse d'épée]]'' ("nobility of the sword") to spend six months of each year at the palace, for reasons similar to those of the Japanese shōguns. The nobles were expected to assist the king in his daily duties and state and personal functions, including meals, parties, and, for the privileged, [[:en:Levée (ceremony)|rising from and getting into bed]], bathing, and going to church.
==See also == * [[:en:Dutch missions to Edo|Dutch missions to Edo]] * [[:en:Ryukyuan missions to Edo|Ryukyuan missions to Edo]]

His wife and heir were required to remain in Edo as hostages while he was away. The expenditures necessary to maintain lavish residences in both places, and for the procession to and from Edo, placed financial strains on the daimyo, making them unable to wage war. The frequent travel of the daimyo encouraged road building and the construction of inns and facilities along the routes, generating economic activity.
There were a number of exceptions for certain ''fudai daimyōs'' in the vicinity of Edo, who were allowed to alternate their attendance in Edo every six months instead. Temporary exceptional dispensations were also occasionally granted due to illness or extreme extenuating circumstances.<ref name= Beasley/>
In principle, the ''sankin-kōtai'' was a military service to the shōgun. Each ''daimyō'' was required to furnish a number of soldiers ([[:en:Samurai|Samurai]]) in accordance with the ''[[:en:Kokudaka|Kokudaka]]'' assessment of his domain. These soldiers accompanied the ''daimyō'' on the processions to and from Edo.
With hundreds of ''daimyōs'' entering or leaving Edo each year, {{nihongo|processions|大名行列|daimyō-gyōretsu}} were almost daily occurrences in the shogunal capital. The main routes to the provinces were the ''[[:en:Kaidō|Kaidō]]''. Special lodgings, the {{nihongo|''honjin''|本陣}}, were available to ''daimyōs'' during their travels.
== Effects ==
=== Economic === The expense of maintaining lavish residences in both Edo and their domain as well as the costly annual journeys to and from Edo was disastrous for the daimyo and greatly enhanced the political control imposed by the ''sankin-kōtai'' system.{{sfn|Tsukahira|1966|p=81}} As the main source of money for most daimyo was mainly dependent on the rice surplus, which was sharply limited by the relative inflexibility of the productive capacity of the [[:en:Han system|''han'']], the incomes of the daimyo were unable to keep up with the increasing financial needs of the daimyo, forcing them to go into debt.{{sfn|Tsukahira|1966|pp=83-84}} ''Sankin-kōtai'' expenses regularly made up 70 to 80 percent of the total annual expenditures of daimyo. Economic programs, efforts to increase production, and special taxes were all attempted, but offered no permanent solution.{{sfn|Tsukahira|1966|p=101}} By the [[:en:Genroku|Genroku period]], practically every daimyo was deep in debt due to the extravagant spending universal during this period. Even the wealthiest feudatory, the [[Maeda clan]] of Kaga, began to encounter financial difficulties at this time.{{sfn|Tsukahira|1966|p=85}}
== Similar practices == [[:en:King of France|King]] [[:en:Louis XIV of France|Louis XIV of France]] instituted a similar practice upon the completion of his [[:en:Palace of Versailles|palace at Versailles]], requiring the [[:en:French nobility|French nobility]], particularly the ancient ''[[:en:Noblesse d'épée|Noblesse d'épée]]'' ("nobility of the sword") to spend six months of each year at the palace, for reasons similar to those of the Japanese shōguns. The nobles were expected to assist the king in his daily duties and state and personal functions, including meals, parties, and, for the privileged, [[:en:Levée (ceremony)|rising from and getting into bed]], bathing, and going to church.

[[File:Tokugawa Seiseiroku 001.jpg|thumb|350px| "En masse Attendance of Daimyo at [[:en:Edo Castle|Edo Castle]] on a Festive Day" from the Tokugawa Seiseiroku, [[:en:National Museum of Japanese History|National Museum of Japanese History]]]] '''''Sankin-kōtai''''' ({{langx|ja|参覲交代/参覲交替}}, now commonly written as {{langx|ja|参勤交代/参勤交替|lit=alternate attendance|label=none}}) was a policy of the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] during most of the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], created to control the [[daimyo]], the feudal lords of Japan, politically, and to keep them from attempting to overthrow the regime. It required most daimyo to alternate between living in their domain and in the shogunate's capital, [[:en:Edo|Edo]], every year. This made the daimyo subject to constant surveillance from the shogunate. This also forced the daimyo to have residences in both their domain and Edo. The cost of maintaining several lavish residences as well as the journeys to and from Edo was a constant drain on the finances of the daimyo, which greatly increased the shogunate's control over them and kept them militarily weak. The daimyo were also required to keep their wife and children in Edo permanently to act as hostages.
==History== [[:en:Toyotomi Hideyoshi|Toyotomi Hideyoshi]] had earlier established a similar practice of requiring his feudal lords to keep their wives and heirs at [[:en:Osaka Castle|Osaka Castle]] or the nearby vicinity as hostages to ensure their loyalty. Following the [[:en:Battle of Sekigahara|Battle of Sekigahara]] and the establishment of the Tokugawa Shogunate, this practice was continued at the new capital of Edo as a matter of custom. It was made compulsory for the ''[[:en:Tozama daimyō|Tozama daimyō]]s'' in 1635, and for the ''[[:en:Fudai daimyō|Fudai daimyō]]s'' from 1642. Aside from an eight-year period under the rule of [[:en:Tokugawa Yoshimune|Tokugawa Yoshimune]], the law remained in force until 1862.<ref name= Beasley>{{cite book | last = Beasley| first = William G | year = 1972 | title = The Meiji Restoration| url = https://archive.org/details/meijirestoration00beas| url-access = limited| publisher = Stamford University Press | isbn=0804708150 |pages=[https://archive.org/details/meijirestoration00beas/page/n30 17]–18}}</ref>
===Predecessors=== The ''sankin-kōtai'' system was a natural outgrowth of pre-existing practices which were expanded by the Tokugawa shogunate to further their own political interests.{{sfn|Tsukahira|1966|p=30}} Much of the reason the newly created shogunate could impose ''sankin-kōtai'' on the defeated ''daimyo'' with ease was due to these immediate predecessors.{{sfn|Tsukahira|1966|p=35}} ====Kamakura period==== The Tokugawa period philosopher [[:en:Kumazawa Banzan|Kumazawa Banzan]] wrote in his ''Daigaku wakumon'' that the [[:en:Kamakura shogunate|Kamakura shogunate]] had its own version of alternate attendance, which made ''[[:en:Gokenin|Gokenin]]'' visit the shogun's court in [[:en:Kamakura|Kamakura]] for fifty days once every three years. Kumazawa claimed that his account was based on records preserved by the descendants of ancient Kamakura families. Modern scholars have been unable to confirm the existence of such detailed rules, but there is evidence that, at least in principle, Kamakura period ''gokenin'' owed some kind of service at the shogun's court. The obligatory ''ōyuka'' service required ''gokenin'' to attend at the shogun's court for one month. They were also required to attend on special occasions such as comings of age, weddings, and funerals.{{sfn|Tsukahira|1966|pp=31-32}}
The principal duty of the ''gokenin'' was ''{{ill|ōban'yaku|ja|大番役|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/ōban'yaku|1=ōban'yaku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11261246}}'', which was periodic guard duty at [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto]] and Kamakura. It originated as a public duty of the warrior class, requiring performance of guard service in Kyoto in one out of every three years. In 1186, [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]] made this service mandatory for ''gokenin'' and reduced the duration to six months, and then later to three. In 1275, a similar arrangement was instituted at Kamakura, making ''gokenin'' in eastern Japan serve there for one month annually. Important ''gokenin'' who frequently performed guard service there maintained residences in Kamakura, even those from remoter regions in western Japan. It was also frequent practice for ''[[:en:Shugo|Shugo]]'' and ''[[:en:Jitō|Jitō]]'' to have their sons serve in the shogun's court, acting as hostages. In some cases, families were kept in Kamakura.{{sfn|Tsukahira|1966|pp=32-33}}
These obligations on the Kamakura ''gokenin'' had a limiting effect on local independence, like the more rigid ''sankin-kōtai'' system that emerged in the Edo period. Shimmi Kichiji claimed that the prolonged absence of the ''gokenin'' from their fiefs due to their obligatory attendance at the capital delayed the development of true feudalism in Japan.{{sfn|Tsukahira|1966|p=33}}
==Description== [[File:Kyosai Kiyomitsu 001c.jpg|thumb|Sightseers and merchants gazing at an entourage (sixth panel) from "Folding Screen Depicting Scenes of the Attendance of ''Daimyōs'' at Edo Castle", National Museum of Japanese History]] The details changed throughout the 26 decades of Tokugawa rule, but generally, the requirement was that the ''daimyōs'' of every [[:en:Han (Japan)|''han'']] move periodically between Edo and his fief, typically spending alternate years in each place. His wife and heir were required to remain in Edo as hostages while he was away. The expenditures necessary to maintain lavish residences in both places, and for the procession to and from Edo, placed financial strains on the daimyo, making them unable to wage war. The frequent travel of the daimyo encouraged road building and the construction of inns and facilities along the routes, generating economic activity.
There were a number of exceptions for certain ''fudai daimyōs'' in the vicinity of Edo, who were allowed to alternate their attendance in Edo every six months instead. Temporary exceptional dispensations were also occasionally granted due to illness or extreme extenuating circumstances.<ref name= Beasley/>
In principle, the ''sankin-kōtai'' was a military service to the shōgun. Each ''daimyō'' was required to furnish a number of soldiers ([[:en:Samurai|Samurai]]) in accordance with the ''[[:en:Kokudaka|Kokudaka]]'' assessment of his domain. These soldiers accompanied the ''daimyō'' on the processions to and from Edo.
With hundreds of ''daimyōs'' entering or leaving Edo each year, {{nihongo|processions|大名行列|daimyō-gyōretsu}} were almost daily occurrences in the shogunal capital. The main routes to the provinces were the ''[[:en:Kaidō|Kaidō]]''. Special lodgings, the {{nihongo|''honjin''|本陣}}, were available to ''daimyōs'' during their travels.
== Effects ==
=== Economic === The expense of maintaining lavish residences in both Edo and their domain as well as the costly annual journeys to and from Edo was disastrous for the daimyo and greatly enhanced the political control imposed by the ''sankin-kōtai'' system.{{sfn|Tsukahira|1966|p=81}} As the main source of money for most daimyo was mainly dependent on the rice surplus, which was sharply limited by the relative inflexibility of the productive capacity of the [[:en:Han system|''han'']], the incomes of the daimyo were unable to keep up with the increasing financial needs of the daimyo, forcing them to go into debt.{{sfn|Tsukahira|1966|pp=83-84}} ''Sankin-kōtai'' expenses regularly made up 70 to 80 percent of the total annual expenditures of daimyo. Economic programs, efforts to increase production, and special taxes were all attempted, but offered no permanent solution.{{sfn|Tsukahira|1966|p=101}} By the [[:en:Genroku|Genroku period]], practically every daimyo was deep in debt due to the extravagant spending universal during this period. Even the wealthiest feudatory, the [[Maeda clan]] of Kaga, began to encounter financial difficulties at this time.{{sfn|Tsukahira|1966|p=85}}
== Similar practices == [[:en:King of France|King]] [[:en:Louis XIV of France|Louis XIV of France]] instituted a similar practice upon the completion of his [[:en:Palace of Versailles|palace at Versailles]], requiring the [[:en:French nobility|French nobility]], particularly the ancient ''[[:en:Noblesse d'épée|Noblesse d'épée]]'' ("nobility of the sword") to spend six months of each year at the palace, for reasons similar to those of the Japanese shōguns. The nobles were expected to assist the king in his daily duties and state and personal functions, including meals, parties, and, for the privileged, [[:en:Levée (ceremony)|rising from and getting into bed]], bathing, and going to church.

[[File:Tokugawa Seiseiroku 001.jpg|thumb|350px| "En masse Attendance of Daimyo at [[:en:Edo Castle|Edo Castle]] on a Festive Day" from the Tokugawa Seiseiroku, [[:en:National Museum of Japanese History|National Museum of Japanese History]]]] '''''Sankin-kōtai''''' ({{langx|ja|参覲交代/参覲交替}}, now commonly written as {{langx|ja|参勤交代/参勤交替|lit=alternate attendance|label=none}}) was a policy of the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] during most of the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], created to control the [[daimyo]], the feudal lords of Japan, politically, and to keep them from attempting to overthrow the regime. This made the daimyo subject to constant surveillance from the shogunate. This also forced the daimyo to have residences in both their domain and Edo. The daimyo were also required to keep their wife and children in Edo permanently to act as hostages.

Aside from an eight-year period under the rule of [[:en:Tokugawa Yoshimune|Tokugawa Yoshimune]], the law remained in force until 1862.<ref name= Beasley>{{cite book | last = Beasley| first = William G | year = 1972 | title = The Meiji Restoration| url = https://archive.org/details/meijirestoration00beas| url-access = limited| publisher = Stamford University Press | isbn=0804708150 |pages=[https://archive.org/details/meijirestoration00beas/page/n30 17]–18}}</ref>

Modern scholars have been unable to confirm the existence of such detailed rules, but there is evidence that, at least in principle, Kamakura period ''gokenin'' owed some kind of service at the shogun's court. The obligatory ''ōyuka'' service required ''gokenin'' to attend at the shogun's court for one month. They were also required to attend on special occasions such as comings of age, weddings, and funerals.{{sfn|Tsukahira|1966|pp=31-32}}
The principal duty of the ''gokenin'' was ''{{ill|ōban'yaku|ja|大番役|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/ōban'yaku|1=ōban'yaku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11261246}}'', which was periodic guard duty at [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto]] and Kamakura. It originated as a public duty of the warrior class, requiring performance of guard service in Kyoto in one out of every three years. In 1186, [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]] made this service mandatory for ''gokenin'' and reduced the duration to six months, and then later to three. In 1275, a similar arrangement was instituted at Kamakura, making ''gokenin'' in eastern Japan serve there for one month annually. Important ''gokenin'' who frequently performed guard service there maintained residences in Kamakura, even those from remoter regions in western Japan. It was also frequent practice for ''[[:en:Shugo|Shugo]]'' and ''[[:en:Jitō|Jitō]]'' to have their sons serve in the shogun's court, acting as hostages. In some cases, families were kept in Kamakura.{{sfn|Tsukahira|1966|pp=32-33}}
These obligations on the Kamakura ''gokenin'' had a limiting effect on local independence, like the more rigid ''sankin-kōtai'' system that emerged in the Edo period. Shimmi Kichiji claimed that the prolonged absence of the ''gokenin'' from their fiefs due to their obligatory attendance at the capital delayed the development of true feudalism in Japan.{{sfn|Tsukahira|1966|p=33}}
==Description== [[File:Kyosai Kiyomitsu 001c.jpg|thumb|Sightseers and merchants gazing at an entourage (sixth panel) from "Folding Screen Depicting Scenes of the Attendance of ''Daimyōs'' at Edo Castle", National Museum of Japanese History]] The details changed throughout the 26 decades of Tokugawa rule, but generally, the requirement was that the ''daimyōs'' of every [[:en:Han (Japan)|''han'']] move periodically between Edo and his fief, typically spending alternate years in each place. His wife and heir were required to remain in Edo as hostages while he was away. The expenditures necessary to maintain lavish residences in both places, and for the procession to and from Edo, placed financial strains on the daimyo, making them unable to wage war. The frequent travel of the daimyo encouraged road building and the construction of inns and facilities along the routes, generating economic activity.
There were a number of exceptions for certain ''fudai daimyōs'' in the vicinity of Edo, who were allowed to alternate their attendance in Edo every six months instead. Temporary exceptional dispensations were also occasionally granted due to illness or extreme extenuating circumstances.<ref name= Beasley/>
In principle, the ''sankin-kōtai'' was a military service to the shōgun. Each ''daimyō'' was required to furnish a number of soldiers ([[:en:Samurai|Samurai]]) in accordance with the ''[[:en:Kokudaka|Kokudaka]]'' assessment of his domain. These soldiers accompanied the ''daimyō'' on the processions to and from Edo.
With hundreds of ''daimyōs'' entering or leaving Edo each year, {{nihongo|processions|大名行列|daimyō-gyōretsu}} were almost daily occurrences in the shogunal capital. The main routes to the provinces were the ''[[:en:Kaidō|Kaidō]]''. Special lodgings, the {{nihongo|''honjin''|本陣}}, were available to ''daimyōs'' during their travels.
== Effects ==
=== Economic === The expense of maintaining lavish residences in both Edo and their domain as well as the costly annual journeys to and from Edo was disastrous for the daimyo and greatly enhanced the political control imposed by the ''sankin-kōtai'' system.{{sfn|Tsukahira|1966|p=81}} As the main source of money for most daimyo was mainly dependent on the rice surplus, which was sharply limited by the relative inflexibility of the productive capacity of the [[:en:Han system|''han'']], the incomes of the daimyo were unable to keep up with the increasing financial needs of the daimyo, forcing them to go into debt.{{sfn|Tsukahira|1966|pp=83-84}} ''Sankin-kōtai'' expenses regularly made up 70 to 80 percent of the total annual expenditures of daimyo. Economic programs, efforts to increase production, and special taxes were all attempted, but offered no permanent solution.{{sfn|Tsukahira|1966|p=101}} By the [[:en:Genroku|Genroku period]], practically every daimyo was deep in debt due to the extravagant spending universal during this period. Even the wealthiest feudatory, the [[Maeda clan]] of Kaga, began to encounter financial difficulties at this time.{{sfn|Tsukahira|1966|p=85}}
== Similar practices == [[:en:King of France|King]] [[:en:Louis XIV of France|Louis XIV of France]] instituted a similar practice upon the completion of his [[:en:Palace of Versailles|palace at Versailles]], requiring the [[:en:French nobility|French nobility]], particularly the ancient ''[[:en:Noblesse d'épée|Noblesse d'épée]]'' ("nobility of the sword") to spend six months of each year at the palace, for reasons similar to those of the Japanese shōguns. The nobles were expected to assist the king in his daily duties and state and personal functions, including meals, parties, and, for the privileged, [[:en:Levée (ceremony)|rising from and getting into bed]], bathing, and going to church.
==See also == * [[:en:Dutch missions to Edo|Dutch missions to Edo]] * [[:en:Ryukyuan missions to Edo|Ryukyuan missions to Edo]]

[[File:Tokugawa Seiseiroku 001.jpg|thumb|350px| "En masse Attendance of Daimyo at {{ill|Edo Castle|en|Edo Castle|WD=Q865913|lt=Edo Castle}} on a Festive Day" from the Tokugawa Seiseiroku, {{ill|National Museum of Japanese History|en|National Museum of Japanese History|WD=Q3330690|lt=National Museum of Japanese History}}]] '''''Sankin-kōtai''''' ({{langx|ja|参覲交代/参覲交替}}, now commonly written as {{langx|ja|参勤交代/参勤交替|lit=alternate attendance|label=none}}) was a policy of the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] during most of the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period}}, created to control the [[daimyo]], the feudal lords of Japan, politically, and to keep them from attempting to overthrow the regime. It required most daimyo to alternate between living in their domain and in the shogunate's capital, {{ill|Edo|en|Edo|WD=Q215646|lt=Edo}}, every year. This made the daimyo subject to constant surveillance from the shogunate. This also forced the daimyo to have residences in both their domain and Edo. The cost of maintaining several lavish residences as well as the journeys to and from Edo was a constant drain on the finances of the daimyo, which greatly increased the shogunate's control over them and kept them militarily weak. The daimyo were also required to keep their wife and children in Edo permanently to act as hostages.
==History== {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|en|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|WD=Q187550|lt=Toyotomi Hideyoshi}} had earlier established a similar practice of requiring his feudal lords to keep their wives and heirs at {{ill|Osaka Castle|en|Osaka Castle|WD=Q321242|lt=Osaka Castle}} or the nearby vicinity as hostages to ensure their loyalty. Following the {{ill|Battle of Sekigahara|en|Battle of Sekigahara|WD=Q234188|lt=Battle of Sekigahara}} and the establishment of the Tokugawa Shogunate, this practice was continued at the new capital of Edo as a matter of custom. It was made compulsory for the ''{{ill|Tozama daimyō|en|Tozama daimyō|WD=Q1195912|lt=Tozama daimyō}}s'' in 1635, and for the ''{{ill|Fudai daimyō|en|Fudai daimyō|WD=Q643640|lt=Fudai daimyō}}s'' from 1642. Aside from an eight-year period under the rule of {{ill|Tokugawa Yoshimune|en|Tokugawa Yoshimune|WD=Q319664|lt=Tokugawa Yoshimune}}, the law remained in force until 1862.<ref name= Beasley>{{cite book | last = Beasley| first = William G | year = 1972 | title = The Meiji Restoration| url = https://archive.org/details/meijirestoration00beas| url-access = limited| publisher = Stamford University Press | isbn=0804708150 |pages=[https://archive.org/details/meijirestoration00beas/page/n30 17]–18}}</ref>
===Predecessors=== The ''sankin-kōtai'' system was a natural outgrowth of pre-existing practices which were expanded by the Tokugawa shogunate to further their own political interests.{{sfn|Tsukahira|1966|p=30}} Much of the reason the newly created shogunate could impose ''sankin-kōtai'' on the defeated ''daimyo'' with ease was due to these immediate predecessors.{{sfn|Tsukahira|1966|p=35}} ====Kamakura period==== The Tokugawa period philosopher {{ill|Kumazawa Banzan|en|Kumazawa Banzan|WD=Q1037304|lt=Kumazawa Banzan}} wrote in his ''Daigaku wakumon'' that the {{ill|Kamakura shogunate|en|Kamakura shogunate|WD=Q736839|lt=Kamakura shogunate}} had its own version of alternate attendance, which made ''{{ill|Gokenin|en|Gokenin|WD=Q63765|lt=Gokenin}}'' visit the shogun's court in {{ill|Kamakura|en|Kamakura|WD=Q200267|lt=Kamakura}} for fifty days once every three years. Kumazawa claimed that his account was based on records preserved by the descendants of ancient Kamakura families. Modern scholars have been unable to confirm the existence of such detailed rules, but there is evidence that, at least in principle, Kamakura period ''gokenin'' owed some kind of service at the shogun's court. The obligatory ''ōyuka'' service required ''gokenin'' to attend at the shogun's court for one month. They were also required to attend on special occasions such as comings of age, weddings, and funerals.{{sfn|Tsukahira|1966|pp=31-32}}
The principal duty of the ''gokenin'' was ''{{ill|ōban'yaku|ja|大番役|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/ōban'yaku|1=ōban'yaku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11261246}}'', which was periodic guard duty at {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|WD=Q34600|lt=Kyoto}} and Kamakura. It originated as a public duty of the warrior class, requiring performance of guard service in Kyoto in one out of every three years. In 1186, [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]] made this service mandatory for ''gokenin'' and reduced the duration to six months, and then later to three. In 1275, a similar arrangement was instituted at Kamakura, making ''gokenin'' in eastern Japan serve there for one month annually. Important ''gokenin'' who frequently performed guard service there maintained residences in Kamakura, even those from remoter regions in western Japan. It was also frequent practice for ''{{ill|Shugo|en|Shugo|WD=Q1141023|lt=Shugo}}'' and ''{{ill|Jitō|en|Jitō|WD=Q1329805|lt=Jitō}}'' to have their sons serve in the shogun's court, acting as hostages. In some cases, families were kept in Kamakura.{{sfn|Tsukahira|1966|pp=32-33}}
These obligations on the Kamakura ''gokenin'' had a limiting effect on local independence, like the more rigid ''sankin-kōtai'' system that emerged in the Edo period. Shimmi Kichiji claimed that the prolonged absence of the ''gokenin'' from their fiefs due to their obligatory attendance at the capital delayed the development of true feudalism in Japan.{{sfn|Tsukahira|1966|p=33}}
==Description== [[File:Kyosai Kiyomitsu 001c.jpg|thumb|Sightseers and merchants gazing at an entourage (sixth panel) from "Folding Screen Depicting Scenes of the Attendance of ''Daimyōs'' at Edo Castle", National Museum of Japanese History]] The details changed throughout the 26 decades of Tokugawa rule, but generally, the requirement was that the ''daimyōs'' of every [[:en:Han (Japan)|''han'']] move periodically between Edo and his fief, typically spending alternate years in each place. His wife and heir were required to remain in Edo as hostages while he was away. The expenditures necessary to maintain lavish residences in both places, and for the procession to and from Edo, placed financial strains on the daimyo, making them unable to wage war. The frequent travel of the daimyo encouraged road building and the construction of inns and facilities along the routes, generating economic activity.
There were a number of exceptions for certain ''fudai daimyōs'' in the vicinity of Edo, who were allowed to alternate their attendance in Edo every six months instead. Temporary exceptional dispensations were also occasionally granted due to illness or extreme extenuating circumstances.<ref name= Beasley/>
In principle, the ''sankin-kōtai'' was a military service to the shōgun. Each ''daimyō'' was required to furnish a number of soldiers ({{ill|Samurai|en|Samurai|WD=Q38142|lt=Samurai}}) in accordance with the ''{{ill|Kokudaka|en|Kokudaka|WD=Q909302|lt=Kokudaka}}'' assessment of his domain. These soldiers accompanied the ''daimyō'' on the processions to and from Edo.
With hundreds of ''daimyōs'' entering or leaving Edo each year, {{nihongo|processions|大名行列|daimyō-gyōretsu}} were almost daily occurrences in the shogunal capital. The main routes to the provinces were the ''{{ill|Kaidō|en|Kaidō|WD=Q749827|lt=Kaidō}}''. Special lodgings, the {{nihongo|''honjin''|本陣}}, were available to ''daimyōs'' during their travels.
== Effects ==
=== Economic === The expense of maintaining lavish residences in both Edo and their domain as well as the costly annual journeys to and from Edo was disastrous for the daimyo and greatly enhanced the political control imposed by the ''sankin-kōtai'' system.{{sfn|Tsukahira|1966|p=81}} As the main source of money for most daimyo was mainly dependent on the rice surplus, which was sharply limited by the relative inflexibility of the productive capacity of the {{ill|Han system|en|Han system|WD=Q841985|lt=''han''}}, the incomes of the daimyo were unable to keep up with the increasing financial needs of the daimyo, forcing them to go into debt.{{sfn|Tsukahira|1966|pp=83-84}} ''Sankin-kōtai'' expenses regularly made up 70 to 80 percent of the total annual expenditures of daimyo. Economic programs, efforts to increase production, and special taxes were all attempted, but offered no permanent solution.{{sfn|Tsukahira|1966|p=101}} By the {{ill|Genroku|en|Genroku|WD=Q1325001|lt=Genroku period}}, practically every daimyo was deep in debt due to the extravagant spending universal during this period. Even the wealthiest feudatory, the [[Maeda clan]] of Kaga, began to encounter financial difficulties at this time.{{sfn|Tsukahira|1966|p=85}}
== Similar practices == [[:en:King of France|King]] [[:en:Louis XIV of France|Louis XIV of France]] instituted a similar practice upon the completion of his {{ill|Palace of Versailles|en|Palace of Versailles|WD=Q2946|lt=palace at Versailles}}, requiring the {{ill|French nobility|en|French nobility|WD=Q1450700|lt=French nobility}}, particularly the ancient ''[[:en:Noblesse d'épée|Noblesse d'épée]]'' ("nobility of the sword") to spend six months of each year at the palace, for reasons similar to those of the Japanese shōguns. The nobles were expected to assist the king in his daily duties and state and personal functions, including meals, parties, and, for the privileged, [[:en:Levée (ceremony)|rising from and getting into bed]], bathing, and going to church.

[[File:Tokugawa Seiseiroku 001.jpg|thumb|350px| "En masse Attendance of Daimyo at {{ill|Edo Castle|en|Edo Castle|WD=Q865913|lt=Edo Castle}} on a Festive Day" from the Tokugawa Seiseiroku, {{ill|National Museum of Japanese History|en|National Museum of Japanese History|WD=Q3330690|lt=National Museum of Japanese History}}]] '''''Sankin-kōtai''''' ({{langx|ja|参覲交代/参覲交替}}, now commonly written as {{langx|ja|参勤交代/参勤交替|lit=alternate attendance|label=none}}) was a policy of the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] during most of the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period}}, created to control the [[daimyo]], the feudal lords of Japan, politically, and to keep them from attempting to overthrow the regime. It required most daimyo to alternate between living in their domain and in the shogunate's capital, {{ill|Edo|en|Edo|WD=Q215646|lt=Edo}}, every year. This made the daimyo subject to constant surveillance from the shogunate. This also forced the daimyo to have residences in both their domain and Edo. The cost of maintaining several lavish residences as well as the journeys to and from Edo was a constant drain on the finances of the daimyo, which greatly increased the shogunate's control over them and kept them militarily weak. The daimyo were also required to keep their wife and children in Edo permanently to act as hostages.
==History== {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|en|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|WD=Q187550|lt=Toyotomi Hideyoshi}} had earlier established a similar practice of requiring his feudal lords to keep their wives and heirs at {{ill|Osaka Castle|en|Osaka Castle|WD=Q321242|lt=Osaka Castle}} or the nearby vicinity as hostages to ensure their loyalty. Following the {{ill|Battle of Sekigahara|en|Battle of Sekigahara|WD=Q234188|lt=Battle of Sekigahara}} and the establishment of the Tokugawa Shogunate, this practice was continued at the new capital of Edo as a matter of custom. It was made compulsory for the ''{{ill|Tozama daimyō|en|Tozama daimyō|WD=Q1195912|lt=Tozama daimyō}}s'' in 1635, and for the ''{{ill|Fudai daimyō|en|Fudai daimyō|WD=Q643640|lt=Fudai daimyō}}s'' from 1642. Aside from an eight-year period under the rule of {{ill|Tokugawa Yoshimune|en|Tokugawa Yoshimune|WD=Q319664|lt=Tokugawa Yoshimune}}, the law remained in force until 1862.<ref name= Beasley>{{cite book | last = Beasley| first = William G | year = 1972 | title = The Meiji Restoration| url = https://archive.org/details/meijirestoration00beas| url-access = limited| publisher = Stamford University Press | isbn=0804708150 |pages=[https://archive.org/details/meijirestoration00beas/page/n30 17]–18}}</ref>
===Predecessors=== The ''sankin-kōtai'' system was a natural outgrowth of pre-existing practices which were expanded by the Tokugawa shogunate to further their own political interests.{{sfn|Tsukahira|1966|p=30}} Much of the reason the newly created shogunate could impose ''sankin-kōtai'' on the defeated ''daimyo'' with ease was due to these immediate predecessors.{{sfn|Tsukahira|1966|p=35}} ====Kamakura period==== The Tokugawa period philosopher {{ill|Kumazawa Banzan|en|Kumazawa Banzan|WD=Q1037304|lt=Kumazawa Banzan}} wrote in his ''Daigaku wakumon'' that the {{ill|Kamakura shogunate|en|Kamakura shogunate|WD=Q736839|lt=Kamakura shogunate}} had its own version of alternate attendance, which made ''{{ill|Gokenin|en|Gokenin|WD=Q63765|lt=Gokenin}}'' visit the shogun's court in {{ill|Kamakura|en|Kamakura|WD=Q200267|lt=Kamakura}} for fifty days once every three years. Kumazawa claimed that his account was based on records preserved by the descendants of ancient Kamakura families. Modern scholars have been unable to confirm the existence of such detailed rules, but there is evidence that, at least in principle, Kamakura period ''gokenin'' owed some kind of service at the shogun's court. The obligatory ''ōyuka'' service required ''gokenin'' to attend at the shogun's court for one month. They were also required to attend on special occasions such as comings of age, weddings, and funerals.{{sfn|Tsukahira|1966|pp=31-32}}
The principal duty of the ''gokenin'' was ''{{ill|ōban'yaku|ja|大番役|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/ōban'yaku|1=ōban'yaku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11261246}}'', which was periodic guard duty at {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|WD=Q34600|lt=Kyoto}} and Kamakura. It originated as a public duty of the warrior class, requiring performance of guard service in Kyoto in one out of every three years. In 1186, [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]] made this service mandatory for ''gokenin'' and reduced the duration to six months, and then later to three. In 1275, a similar arrangement was instituted at Kamakura, making ''gokenin'' in eastern Japan serve there for one month annually. Important ''gokenin'' who frequently performed guard service there maintained residences in Kamakura, even those from remoter regions in western Japan. It was also frequent practice for ''{{ill|Shugo|en|Shugo|WD=Q1141023|lt=Shugo}}'' and ''{{ill|Jitō|en|Jitō|WD=Q1329805|lt=Jitō}}'' to have their sons serve in the shogun's court, acting as hostages. In some cases, families were kept in Kamakura.{{sfn|Tsukahira|1966|pp=32-33}}
These obligations on the Kamakura ''gokenin'' had a limiting effect on local independence, like the more rigid ''sankin-kōtai'' system that emerged in the Edo period. Shimmi Kichiji claimed that the prolonged absence of the ''gokenin'' from their fiefs due to their obligatory attendance at the capital delayed the development of true feudalism in Japan.{{sfn|Tsukahira|1966|p=33}}
==Description== [[File:Kyosai Kiyomitsu 001c.jpg|thumb|Sightseers and merchants gazing at an entourage (sixth panel) from "Folding Screen Depicting Scenes of the Attendance of ''Daimyōs'' at Edo Castle", National Museum of Japanese History]] The details changed throughout the 26 decades of Tokugawa rule, but generally, the requirement was that the ''daimyōs'' of every [[:en:Han (Japan)|''han'']] move periodically between Edo and his fief, typically spending alternate years in each place. His wife and heir were required to remain in Edo as hostages while he was away. The expenditures necessary to maintain lavish residences in both places, and for the procession to and from Edo, placed financial strains on the daimyo, making them unable to wage war. The frequent travel of the daimyo encouraged road building and the construction of inns and facilities along the routes, generating economic activity.
There were a number of exceptions for certain ''fudai daimyōs'' in the vicinity of Edo, who were allowed to alternate their attendance in Edo every six months instead. Temporary exceptional dispensations were also occasionally granted due to illness or extreme extenuating circumstances.<ref name= Beasley/>
In principle, the ''sankin-kōtai'' was a military service to the shōgun. Each ''daimyō'' was required to furnish a number of soldiers ({{ill|Samurai|en|Samurai|WD=Q38142|lt=Samurai}}) in accordance with the ''{{ill|Kokudaka|en|Kokudaka|WD=Q909302|lt=Kokudaka}}'' assessment of his domain. These soldiers accompanied the ''daimyō'' on the processions to and from Edo.
With hundreds of ''daimyōs'' entering or leaving Edo each year, {{nihongo|processions|大名行列|daimyō-gyōretsu}} were almost daily occurrences in the shogunal capital. The main routes to the provinces were the ''{{ill|Kaidō|en|Kaidō|WD=Q749827|lt=Kaidō}}''. Special lodgings, the {{nihongo|''honjin''|本陣}}, were available to ''daimyōs'' during their travels.
== Effects ==
=== Economic === The expense of maintaining lavish residences in both Edo and their domain as well as the costly annual journeys to and from Edo was disastrous for the daimyo and greatly enhanced the political control imposed by the ''sankin-kōtai'' system.{{sfn|Tsukahira|1966|p=81}} As the main source of money for most daimyo was mainly dependent on the rice surplus, which was sharply limited by the relative inflexibility of the productive capacity of the {{ill|Han system|en|Han system|WD=Q841985|lt=''han''}}, the incomes of the daimyo were unable to keep up with the increasing financial needs of the daimyo, forcing them to go into debt.{{sfn|Tsukahira|1966|pp=83-84}} ''Sankin-kōtai'' expenses regularly made up 70 to 80 percent of the total annual expenditures of daimyo. Economic programs, efforts to increase production, and special taxes were all attempted, but offered no permanent solution.{{sfn|Tsukahira|1966|p=101}} By the {{ill|Genroku|en|Genroku|WD=Q1325001|lt=Genroku period}}, practically every daimyo was deep in debt due to the extravagant spending universal during this period. Even the wealthiest feudatory, the [[Maeda clan]] of Kaga, began to encounter financial difficulties at this time.{{sfn|Tsukahira|1966|p=85}}
== Similar practices == [[:en:King of France|King]] [[:en:Louis XIV of France|Louis XIV of France]] instituted a similar practice upon the completion of his {{ill|Palace of Versailles|en|Palace of Versailles|WD=Q2946|lt=palace at Versailles}}, requiring the {{ill|French nobility|en|French nobility|WD=Q1450700|lt=French nobility}}, particularly the ancient ''[[:en:Noblesse d'épée|Noblesse d'épée]]'' ("nobility of the sword") to spend six months of each year at the palace, for reasons similar to those of the Japanese shōguns. The nobles were expected to assist the king in his daily duties and state and personal functions, including meals, parties, and, for the privileged, [[:en:Levée (ceremony)|rising from and getting into bed]], bathing, and going to church.
==See also == * {{ill|Dutch missions to Edo|en|Dutch missions to Edo|WD=Q1411432|lt=Dutch missions to Edo}} * {{ill|Ryukyuan missions to Edo|en|Ryukyuan missions to Edo|WD=Q1589309|lt=Ryukyuan missions to Edo}}

Temporary exceptional dispensations were also occasionally granted due to illness or extreme extenuating circumstances.<ref name= Beasley/>
In principle, the ''sankin-kōtai'' was a military service to the shōgun. Each ''daimyō'' was required to furnish a number of soldiers ({{ill|Samurai|en|Samurai|WD=Q38142|lt=Samurai}}) in accordance with the ''{{ill|Kokudaka|en|Kokudaka|WD=Q909302|lt=Kokudaka}}'' assessment of his domain. These soldiers accompanied the ''daimyō'' on the processions to and from Edo.
With hundreds of ''daimyōs'' entering or leaving Edo each year, {{nihongo|processions|大名行列|daimyō-gyōretsu}} were almost daily occurrences in the shogunal capital. The main routes to the provinces were the ''{{ill|Kaidō|en|Kaidō|WD=Q749827|lt=Kaidō}}''. Special lodgings, the {{nihongo|''honjin''|本陣}}, were available to ''daimyōs'' during their travels.
== Effects ==
=== Economic === The expense of maintaining lavish residences in both Edo and their domain as well as the costly annual journeys to and from Edo was disastrous for the daimyo and greatly enhanced the political control imposed by the ''sankin-kōtai'' system.{{sfn|Tsukahira|1966|p=81}} As the main source of money for most daimyo was mainly dependent on the rice surplus, which was sharply limited by the relative inflexibility of the productive capacity of the {{ill|Han system|en|Han system|WD=Q841985|lt=''han''}}, the incomes of the daimyo were unable to keep up with the increasing financial needs of the daimyo, forcing them to go into debt.{{sfn|Tsukahira|1966|pp=83-84}} ''Sankin-kōtai'' expenses regularly made up 70 to 80 percent of the total annual expenditures of daimyo. Economic programs, efforts to increase production, and special taxes were all attempted, but offered no permanent solution.{{sfn|Tsukahira|1966|p=101}} By the {{ill|Genroku|en|Genroku|WD=Q1325001|lt=Genroku period}}, practically every daimyo was deep in debt due to the extravagant spending universal during this period. Even the wealthiest feudatory, the [[Maeda clan]] of Kaga, began to encounter financial difficulties at this time.{{sfn|Tsukahira|1966|p=85}}
== Similar practices == [[:en:King of France|King]] [[:en:Louis XIV of France|Louis XIV of France]] instituted a similar practice upon the completion of his {{ill|Palace of Versailles|en|Palace of Versailles|WD=Q2946|lt=palace at Versailles}}, requiring the {{ill|French nobility|en|French nobility|WD=Q1450700|lt=French nobility}}, particularly the ancient ''[[:en:Noblesse d'épée|Noblesse d'épée]]'' ("nobility of the sword") to spend six months of each year at the palace, for reasons similar to those of the Japanese shōguns. The nobles were expected to assist the king in his daily duties and state and personal functions, including meals, parties, and, for the privileged, [[:en:Levée (ceremony)|rising from and getting into bed]], bathing, and going to church.
==See also == * {{ill|Dutch missions to Edo|en|Dutch missions to Edo|WD=Q1411432|lt=Dutch missions to Edo}} * {{ill|Ryukyuan missions to Edo|en|Ryukyuan missions to Edo|WD=Q1589309|lt=Ryukyuan missions to Edo}}

[[File:Tokugawa Seiseiroku 001.jpg|thumb|350px| "En masse Attendance of Daimyo at {{ill|Edo Castle|en|Edo Castle|WD=Q865913|lt=Edo Castle}} on a Festive Day" from the Tokugawa Seiseiroku, {{ill|National Museum of Japanese History|en|National Museum of Japanese History|WD=Q3330690|lt=National Museum of Japanese History}}]] '''''Sankin-kōtai''''' ({{langx|ja|参覲交代/参覲交替}}, now commonly written as {{langx|ja|参勤交代/参勤交替|lit=alternate attendance|label=none}}) was a policy of the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] during most of the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period}}, created to control the [[daimyo]], the feudal lords of Japan, politically, and to keep them from attempting to overthrow the regime. It required most daimyo to alternate between living in their domain and in the shogunate's capital, {{ill|Edo|en|Edo|WD=Q215646|lt=Edo}}, every year. This made the daimyo subject to constant surveillance from the shogunate. This also forced the daimyo to have residences in both their domain and Edo. The cost of maintaining several lavish residences as well as the journeys to and from Edo was a constant drain on the finances of the daimyo, which greatly increased the shogunate's control over them and kept them militarily weak. The daimyo were also required to keep their wife and children in Edo permanently to act as hostages.
==History== {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|en|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|WD=Q187550|lt=Toyotomi Hideyoshi}} had earlier established a similar practice of requiring his feudal lords to keep their wives and heirs at {{ill|Osaka Castle|en|Osaka Castle|WD=Q321242|lt=Osaka Castle}} or the nearby vicinity as hostages to ensure their loyalty. Following the {{ill|Battle of Sekigahara|en|Battle of Sekigahara|WD=Q234188|lt=Battle of Sekigahara}} and the establishment of the Tokugawa Shogunate, this practice was continued at the new capital of Edo as a matter of custom. It was made compulsory for the ''{{ill|Tozama daimyō|en|Tozama daimyō|WD=Q1195912|lt=Tozama daimyō}}s'' in 1635, and for the ''{{ill|Fudai daimyō|en|Fudai daimyō|WD=Q643640|lt=Fudai daimyō}}s'' from 1642. Aside from an eight-year period under the rule of {{ill|Tokugawa Yoshimune|en|Tokugawa Yoshimune|WD=Q319664|lt=Tokugawa Yoshimune}}, the law remained in force until 1862.<ref name= Beasley>{{cite book | last = Beasley| first = William G | year = 1972 | title = The Meiji Restoration| url = https://archive.org/details/meijirestoration00beas| url-access = limited| publisher = Stamford University Press | isbn=0804708150 |pages=[https://archive.org/details/meijirestoration00beas/page/n30 17]–18}}</ref>
===Predecessors=== The ''sankin-kōtai'' system was a natural outgrowth of pre-existing practices which were expanded by the Tokugawa shogunate to further their own political interests.{{sfn|Tsukahira|1966|p=30}} Much of the reason the newly created shogunate could impose ''sankin-kōtai'' on the defeated ''daimyo'' with ease was due to these immediate predecessors.{{sfn|Tsukahira|1966|p=35}} ====Kamakura period==== The Tokugawa period philosopher {{ill|Kumazawa Banzan|en|Kumazawa Banzan|WD=Q1037304|lt=Kumazawa Banzan}} wrote in his ''Daigaku wakumon'' that the {{ill|Kamakura shogunate|en|Kamakura shogunate|WD=Q736839|lt=Kamakura shogunate}} had its own version of alternate attendance, which made ''{{ill|Gokenin|en|Gokenin|WD=Q63765|lt=Gokenin}}'' visit the shogun's court in {{ill|Kamakura|en|Kamakura|WD=Q200267|lt=Kamakura}} for fifty days once every three years. Kumazawa claimed that his account was based on records preserved by the descendants of ancient Kamakura families. Modern scholars have been unable to confirm the existence of such detailed rules, but there is evidence that, at least in principle, Kamakura period ''gokenin'' owed some kind of service at the shogun's court. The obligatory ''ōyuka'' service required ''gokenin'' to attend at the shogun's court for one month. They were also required to attend on special occasions such as comings of age, weddings, and funerals.{{sfn|Tsukahira|1966|pp=31-32}}
The principal duty of the ''gokenin'' was ''{{ill|ōban'yaku|ja|大番役|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/ōban'yaku|1=ōban'yaku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11261246}}'', which was periodic guard duty at {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|WD=Q34600|lt=Kyoto}} and Kamakura. It originated as a public duty of the warrior class, requiring performance of guard service in Kyoto in one out of every three years. In 1186, [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]] made this service mandatory for ''gokenin'' and reduced the duration to six months, and then later to three. In 1275, a similar arrangement was instituted at Kamakura, making ''gokenin'' in eastern Japan serve there for one month annually. Important ''gokenin'' who frequently performed guard service there maintained residences in Kamakura, even those from remoter regions in western Japan. It was also frequent practice for ''{{ill|Shugo|en|Shugo|WD=Q1141023|lt=Shugo}}'' and ''{{ill|Jitō|en|Jitō|WD=Q1329805|lt=Jitō}}'' to have their sons serve in the shogun's court, acting as hostages. In some cases, families were kept in Kamakura.{{sfn|Tsukahira|1966|pp=32-33}}
These obligations on the Kamakura ''gokenin'' had a limiting effect on local independence, like the more rigid ''sankin-kōtai'' system that emerged in the Edo period. Shimmi Kichiji claimed that the prolonged absence of the ''gokenin'' from their fiefs due to their obligatory attendance at the capital delayed the development of true feudalism in Japan.{{sfn|Tsukahira|1966|p=33}}
==Description== [[File:Kyosai Kiyomitsu 001c.jpg|thumb|Sightseers and merchants gazing at an entourage (sixth panel) from "Folding Screen Depicting Scenes of the Attendance of ''Daimyōs'' at Edo Castle", National Museum of Japanese History]] The details changed throughout the 26 decades of Tokugawa rule, but generally, the requirement was that the ''daimyōs'' of every [[:en:Han (Japan)|''han'']] move periodically between Edo and his fief, typically spending alternate years in each place. His wife and heir were required to remain in Edo as hostages while he was away. The expenditures necessary to maintain lavish residences in both places, and for the procession to and from Edo, placed financial strains on the daimyo, making them unable to wage war. The frequent travel of the daimyo encouraged road building and the construction of inns and facilities along the routes, generating economic activity.
There were a number of exceptions for certain ''fudai daimyōs'' in the vicinity of Edo, who were allowed to alternate their attendance in Edo every six months instead. Temporary exceptional dispensations were also occasionally granted due to illness or extreme extenuating circumstances.<ref name= Beasley/>
In principle, the ''sankin-kōtai'' was a military service to the shōgun. Each ''daimyō'' was required to furnish a number of soldiers ({{ill|Samurai|en|Samurai|WD=Q38142|lt=Samurai}}) in accordance with the ''{{ill|Kokudaka|en|Kokudaka|WD=Q909302|lt=Kokudaka}}'' assessment of his domain. These soldiers accompanied the ''daimyō'' on the processions to and from Edo.
With hundreds of ''daimyōs'' entering or leaving Edo each year, {{nihongo|processions|大名行列|daimyō-gyōretsu}} were almost daily occurrences in the shogunal capital. The main routes to the provinces were the ''{{ill|Kaidō|en|Kaidō|WD=Q749827|lt=Kaidō}}''. Special lodgings, the {{nihongo|''honjin''|本陣}}, were available to ''daimyōs'' during their travels.
== Effects ==
=== Economic === The expense of maintaining lavish residences in both Edo and their domain as well as the costly annual journeys to and from Edo was disastrous for the daimyo and greatly enhanced the political control imposed by the ''sankin-kōtai'' system.{{sfn|Tsukahira|1966|p=81}} As the main source of money for most daimyo was mainly dependent on the rice surplus, which was sharply limited by the relative inflexibility of the productive capacity of the {{ill|Han system|en|Han system|WD=Q841985|lt=''han''}}, the incomes of the daimyo were unable to keep up with the increasing financial needs of the daimyo, forcing them to go into debt.{{sfn|Tsukahira|1966|pp=83-84}} ''Sankin-kōtai'' expenses regularly made up 70 to 80 percent of the total annual expenditures of daimyo. Economic programs, efforts to increase production, and special taxes were all attempted, but offered no permanent solution.{{sfn|Tsukahira|1966|p=101}} By the {{ill|Genroku|en|Genroku|WD=Q1325001|lt=Genroku period}}, practically every daimyo was deep in debt due to the extravagant spending universal during this period. Even the wealthiest feudatory, the [[Maeda clan]] of Kaga, began to encounter financial difficulties at this time.{{sfn|Tsukahira|1966|p=85}}
== Similar practices == [[:en:King of France|King]] [[:en:Louis XIV of France|Louis XIV of France]] instituted a similar practice upon the completion of his {{ill|Palace of Versailles|en|Palace of Versailles|WD=Q2946|lt=palace at Versailles}}, requiring the {{ill|French nobility|en|French nobility|WD=Q1450700|lt=French nobility}}, particularly the ancient ''[[:en:Noblesse d'épée|Noblesse d'épée]]'' ("nobility of the sword") to spend six months of each year at the palace, for reasons similar to those of the Japanese shōguns. The nobles were expected to assist the king in his daily duties and state and personal functions, including meals, parties, and, for the privileged, [[:en:Levée (ceremony)|rising from and getting into bed]], bathing, and going to church.
==See also == * {{ill|Dutch missions to Edo|en|Dutch missions to Edo|WD=Q1411432|lt=Dutch missions to Edo}} * {{ill|Ryukyuan missions to Edo|en|Ryukyuan missions to Edo|WD=Q1589309|lt=Ryukyuan missions to Edo}}

[[File:Tokugawa Seiseiroku 001.jpg|thumb|350px| "En masse Attendance of Daimyo at {{ill|Edo Castle|en|Edo Castle|WD=Q865913|lt=Edo Castle}} on a Festive Day" from the Tokugawa Seiseiroku, {{ill|National Museum of Japanese History|en|National Museum of Japanese History|WD=Q3330690|lt=National Museum of Japanese History}}]] '''''Sankin-kōtai''''' ({{langx|ja|参覲交代/参覲交替}}, now commonly written as {{langx|ja|参勤交代/参勤交替|lit=alternate attendance|label=none}}) was a policy of the [[Tokugawa shogunate]] during most of the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period}}, created to control the [[daimyo]], the feudal lords of Japan, politically, and to keep them from attempting to overthrow the regime. It required most daimyo to alternate between living in their domain and in the shogunate's capital, {{ill|Edo|en|Edo|WD=Q215646|lt=Edo}}, every year. This made the daimyo subject to constant surveillance from the shogunate. This also forced the daimyo to have residences in both their domain and Edo. The cost of maintaining several lavish residences as well as the journeys to and from Edo was a constant drain on the finances of the daimyo, which greatly increased the shogunate's control over them and kept them militarily weak. The daimyo were also required to keep their wife and children in Edo permanently to act as hostages.
==History== {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|en|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|WD=Q187550|lt=Toyotomi Hideyoshi}} had earlier established a similar practice of requiring his feudal lords to keep their wives and heirs at {{ill|Osaka Castle|en|Osaka Castle|WD=Q321242|lt=Osaka Castle}} or the nearby vicinity as hostages to ensure their loyalty. Following the {{ill|Battle of Sekigahara|en|Battle of Sekigahara|WD=Q234188|lt=Battle of Sekigahara}} and the establishment of the Tokugawa Shogunate, this practice was continued at the new capital of Edo as a matter of custom. It was made compulsory for the ''{{ill|Tozama daimyō|en|Tozama daimyō|WD=Q1195912|lt=Tozama daimyō}}s'' in 1635, and for the ''{{ill|Fudai daimyō|en|Fudai daimyō|WD=Q643640|lt=Fudai daimyō}}s'' from 1642. Aside from an eight-year period under the rule of {{ill|Tokugawa Yoshimune|en|Tokugawa Yoshimune|WD=Q319664|lt=Tokugawa Yoshimune}}, the law remained in force until 1862.<ref name= Beasley>{{cite book | last = Beasley| first = William G | year = 1972 | title = The Meiji Restoration| url = https://archive.org/details/meijirestoration00beas| url-access = limited| publisher = Stamford University Press | isbn=0804708150 |pages=[https://archive.org/details/meijirestoration00beas/page/n30 17]–18}}</ref>
===Predecessors=== The ''sankin-kōtai'' system was a natural outgrowth of pre-existing practices which were expanded by the Tokugawa shogunate to further their own political interests.{{sfn|Tsukahira|1966|p=30}} Much of the reason the newly created shogunate could impose ''sankin-kōtai'' on the defeated ''daimyo'' with ease was due to these immediate predecessors.{{sfn|Tsukahira|1966|p=35}} ====Kamakura period==== The Tokugawa period philosopher {{ill|Kumazawa Banzan|en|Kumazawa Banzan|WD=Q1037304|lt=Kumazawa Banzan}} wrote in his ''Daigaku wakumon'' that the {{ill|Kamakura shogunate|en|Kamakura shogunate|WD=Q736839|lt=Kamakura shogunate}} had its own version of alternate attendance, which made ''{{ill|Gokenin|en|Gokenin|WD=Q63765|lt=Gokenin}}'' visit the shogun's court in {{ill|Kamakura|en|Kamakura|WD=Q200267|lt=Kamakura}} for fifty days once every three years. Kumazawa claimed that his account was based on records preserved by the descendants of ancient Kamakura families. Modern scholars have been unable to confirm the existence of such detailed rules, but there is evidence that, at least in principle, Kamakura period ''gokenin'' owed some kind of service at the shogun's court. The obligatory ''ōyuka'' service required ''gokenin'' to attend at the shogun's court for one month. They were also required to attend on special occasions such as comings of age, weddings, and funerals.{{sfn|Tsukahira|1966|pp=31-32}}
The principal duty of the ''gokenin'' was ''{{ill|ōban'yaku|ja|大番役|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/ōban'yaku|1=ōban'yaku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11261246}}'', which was periodic guard duty at {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|WD=Q34600|lt=Kyoto}} and Kamakura. It originated as a public duty of the warrior class, requiring performance of guard service in Kyoto in one out of every three years. In 1186, [[Minamoto no Yoritomo]] made this service mandatory for ''gokenin'' and reduced the duration to six months, and then later to three. In 1275, a similar arrangement was instituted at Kamakura, making ''gokenin'' in eastern Japan serve there for one month annually. Important ''gokenin'' who frequently performed guard service there maintained residences in Kamakura, even those from remoter regions in western Japan. It was also frequent practice for ''{{ill|Shugo|en|Shugo|WD=Q1141023|lt=Shugo}}'' and ''{{ill|Jitō|en|Jitō|WD=Q1329805|lt=Jitō}}'' to have their sons serve in the shogun's court, acting as hostages. In some cases, families were kept in Kamakura.{{sfn|Tsukahira|1966|pp=32-33}}
These obligations on the Kamakura ''gokenin'' had a limiting effect on local independence, like the more rigid ''sankin-kōtai'' system that emerged in the Edo period. Shimmi Kichiji claimed that the prolonged absence of the ''gokenin'' from their fiefs due to their obligatory attendance at the capital delayed the development of true feudalism in Japan.{{sfn|Tsukahira|1966|p=33}}
==Description== [[File:Kyosai Kiyomitsu 001c.jpg|thumb|Sightseers and merchants gazing at an entourage (sixth panel) from "Folding Screen Depicting Scenes of the Attendance of ''Daimyōs'' at Edo Castle", National Museum of Japanese History]] The details changed throughout the 26 decades of Tokugawa rule, but generally, the requirement was that the ''daimyōs'' of every [[:en:Han (Japan)|''han'']] move periodically between Edo and his fief, typically spending alternate years in each place. His wife and heir were required to remain in Edo as hostages while he was away. The expenditures necessary to maintain lavish residences in both places, and for the procession to and from Edo, placed financial strains on the daimyo, making them unable to wage war. The frequent travel of the daimyo encouraged road building and the construction of inns and facilities along the routes, generating economic activity.
There were a number of exceptions for certain ''fudai daimyōs'' in the vicinity of Edo, who were allowed to alternate their attendance in Edo every six months instead. Temporary exceptional dispensations were also occasionally granted due to illness or extreme extenuating circumstances.<ref name= Beasley/>
In principle, the ''sankin-kōtai'' was a military service to the shōgun. Each ''daimyō'' was required to furnish a number of soldiers ({{ill|Samurai|en|Samurai|WD=Q38142|lt=Samurai}}) in accordance with the ''{{ill|Kokudaka|en|Kokudaka|WD=Q909302|lt=Kokudaka}}'' assessment of his domain. These soldiers accompanied the ''daimyō'' on the processions to and from Edo.
With hundreds of ''daimyōs'' entering or leaving Edo each year, {{nihongo|processions|大名行列|daimyō-gyōretsu}} were almost daily occurrences in the shogunal capital. The main routes to the provinces were the ''{{ill|Kaidō|en|Kaidō|WD=Q749827|lt=Kaidō}}''. Special lodgings, the {{nihongo|''honjin''|本陣}}, were available to ''daimyōs'' during their travels.
== Effects ==
=== Economic === The expense of maintaining lavish residences in both Edo and their domain as well as the costly annual journeys to and from Edo was disastrous for the daimyo and greatly enhanced the political control imposed by the ''sankin-kōtai'' system.{{sfn|Tsukahira|1966|p=81}} As the main source of money for most daimyo was mainly dependent on the rice surplus, which was sharply limited by the relative inflexibility of the productive capacity of the {{ill|Han system|en|Han system|WD=Q841985|lt=''han''}}, the incomes of the daimyo were unable to keep up with the increasing financial needs of the daimyo, forcing them to go into debt.{{sfn|Tsukahira|1966|pp=83-84}} ''Sankin-kōtai'' expenses regularly made up 70 to 80 percent of the total annual expenditures of daimyo. Economic programs, efforts to increase production, and special taxes were all attempted, but offered no permanent solution.{{sfn|Tsukahira|1966|p=101}} By the {{ill|Genroku|en|Genroku|WD=Q1325001|lt=Genroku period}}, practically every daimyo was deep in debt due to the extravagant spending universal during this period. Even the wealthiest feudatory, the [[Maeda clan]] of Kaga, began to encounter financial difficulties at this time.{{sfn|Tsukahira|1966|p=85}}
== Similar practices == [[:en:King of France|King]] [[:en:Louis XIV of France|Louis XIV of France]] instituted a similar practice upon the completion of his {{ill|Palace of Versailles|en|Palace of Versailles|WD=Q2946|lt=palace at Versailles}}, requiring the {{ill|French nobility|en|French nobility|WD=Q1450700|lt=French nobility}}, particularly the ancient ''[[:en:Noblesse d'épée|Noblesse d'épée]]'' ("nobility of the sword") to spend six months of each year at the palace, for reasons similar to those of the Japanese shōguns. The nobles were expected to assist the king in his daily duties and state and personal functions, including meals, parties, and, for the privileged, [[:en:Levée (ceremony)|rising from and getting into bed]], bathing, and going to church.
==See also == * {{ill|Dutch missions to Edo|en|Dutch missions to Edo|WD=Q1411432|lt=Dutch missions to Edo}} * {{ill|Ryukyuan missions to Edo|en|Ryukyuan missions to Edo|WD=Q1589309|lt=Ryukyuan missions to Edo}}
